text:
The case for dessert first is actually airtight.

Consider the physics. You sit down hungry. Your stomach is empty, your judgment unclouded, your appreciation at peak capacity. This is the exact moment you should eat the best thing on the table. Instead, tradition demands you fill up on vegetables and protein, arriving at dessert bloated and compromised. You are literally eating cake at your worst.

There is also the risk calculus. What if you get full? What if there is an emergency? A fire drill, a sudden argument, a waiter who moves suspiciously fast with the check. The salad will wait. The salad has no plans. Chocolate lava cake is a timed event.

Children understand this instinctively. We spend years training them out of it, calling it discipline, calling it good habits. But children are just running honest expected-value calculations. If you can only eat one thing, eat the thing you actually want. Adults forgot this because adults also wear uncomfortable shoes voluntarily, so perhaps our judgment should not be trusted.

The nutritional argument is a wash. You were going to eat the dessert anyway. The order does not change the calories. It only changes who wins: you, eating what you love while still capable of tasting it, or the abstract concept of a balanced meal, which has never once thanked you.

Eat dessert first. Life is short. The broccoli has nowhere to be. You, on the other hand, are a person with finite meals ahead of you, and chocolate mousse does not deserve to be an afterthought.

Priorities are just honesty in action.
